description: This Figure presents mechanical property data central to understanding the strengthening mechanisms in as-quenched Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C low-density steels containing 2.59–11.58 wt% Al (3Al, 6Al, 10Al, 12Al). Based on the extracted text, the image likely displays engineering stress–strain curves for 6Al steel subjected to different solution treatment temperatures (1000s, 1100s, 1200s), exhibiting stress levels from approximately 350–450 MPa at low strains rising to 550–650 MPa near 60% strain, alongside a Hall–Petch plot correlating yield strengths of 340–450 MPa with inverse square root grain sizes to derive σ0 = 250.70 MPa and kG = 683.07 MPa·μm^1/2. The figure may additionally show quantitative strengthening contributions—including Al solid solution, grain boundary, ferrite phase, and nanosized κ-carbide precipitation hardening—that explain the yield strength increase from 321 MPa in 3Al to 952 MPa in 12Al steel. As the actual image pixels and caption were unavailable, these details are reconstructed from the paper context and should be visually verified.

Figure 1 presents the microstructural evolution of as-quenched Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C steels (x = 3, 6, 10, 12) using optical and transmission electron microscopy. Panels (a)–(d) are optical micrographs showing that 3Al, 6Al, and 10Al steels are fully austenitic with extensive annealing twins and similar grain sizes of ~80 μm (excluding twin boundaries), whereas 12Al steel exhibits a duplex microstructure containing blocky ferrite (~7 vol%) and significantly refined austenite grains (~40 μm, or ~16 μm when twin boundaries are counted). Panels (e) and (f) are TEM images with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) insets for 10Al and 12Al steels, respectively. The SAED pattern of 10Al shows weak superlattice spots indicating sparse ordered κ-(Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ carbides, while 12Al displays strong superlattice spots together with two sets of modulation contrast, revealing dense nanosized κ-carbide precipitation and lattice modulation caused by solute atom fluctuation. These observations establish that increasing Al content not only refines grains and introduces ferrite but also promotes κ-carbide precipitation during quenching, providing the structural basis for the dramatic yield strength increase in 12Al steel (952 MPa).

Figure 2 examines the grain-size-dependent mechanical response of Fe-26Mn-6Al-1C (6Al) steel across two sub-panels. Panel (a) shows engineering stress–strain curves for specimens subjected to different solid solution treatments, labeled 1000, 1100, and 1200 in the legend. The blue 1000 curve reaches the highest peak stress near 820 MPa, the red 1100 curve exhibits intermediate flow stress, and the black 1200 curve displays the lowest strength but comparatively larger uniform elongation, indicating progressive grain coarsening and softening with increasing solution temperature. Panel (b) plots yield strength versus d^(-1/2) (μm^(-1/2)), with a reversed top axis indicating grain size d (μm). Three experimental square markers at d^(-1/2) ≈ 0.14, 0.20, and 0.26 μm^(-1/2) align with a linear fit, confirming the Hall–Petch relationship. The caption states that a grain size range of 5.38–52 μm with twin boundaries was obtained via different heat treatment schedules. Together, the panels quantify grain-boundary strengthening in the 6Al alloy and support the reported Hall–Petch constants σ0 = 250.70 MPa and kG = 683.07 MPa·μm^(1/2).

This stacked bar chart (Figure 3) illustrates the decomposition of yield strength contributions in as-quenched Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C steels with varying aluminum contents (3Al, 6Al, 10Al, and 12Al). The vertical axis measures yield strength in MPa, and each stacked column represents the sum of five mechanisms identified in the legend: Base, Solid solution by Al, Ferrite, Grain refining, and Carbide. The total yield strength increases monotonically with Al content, from approximately 320 MPa for 3Al to about 380 MPa for 6Al, 470 MPa for 10Al, and 950 MPa for 12Al. At lower Al levels (3Al and 6Al), the strength is governed primarily by the base strength and Al solid-solution hardening. As Al increases to 10Al, modest contributions from ferrite formation, grain refinement, and carbide precipitation appear. In 12Al, carbide strengthening becomes the dominant contribution, accompanied by substantial solid-solution and grain-refinement effects, which together explain the dramatic threefold increase in yield strength compared with the 3Al alloy.
